title: Optimized near-field optical response via adaptive tip illumination
authors: Tao Chen, Wei Wang, Ziyang Gan, Daniel Repp, Jinxin Zhan, Antony George, Henrik Schneidewind, Ulf Peschel, Andrey Turchanin, Volker Deckert
abstract: The performance of tip-enhanced optical microscopy is often limited by inefficient coupling of the excitation field to the plasmonic tip apex, as well as by thermal drift and optical aberrations. Here, we demonstrate that adaptive wavefront shaping based on Zernike mode provides a practical approach to achieving robust near-field optimisation at the tip apex. Using a sequential feedback algorithm, initially using the near-field signal, we narrow the illumination point-spread function and suppress sidelobes. This demonstrates that Zernike-mode control can be used for both aberration correction and field engineering. In tip-enhanced Raman measurements of a Janus MoSSe monolayer, conventional near-field optimisation increases the signal intensity by around 1.4 fold. A second optimisation step based directly on the Raman-band intensity yields a further 5 to 15 fold enhancement, depending on the specific tips used. These results establish a systematic, optics-based strategy for optimising tip fields, providing a transferable framework for improving tip-enhanced and related near-field spectroscopies.
summary: This paper introduces an adaptive optics approach using Zernike modes to optimize the excitation field in tip-enhanced near-field spectroscopy. By employing a sequential feedback loop based on the measured signal, the authors achieve substantial, systematic enhancements in the TERS signal. This establishes a robust, optics-based framework for improving nanoscale optical measurements.
main_problem: The performance of tip-enhanced optical microscopy (TERS) is limited by inefficient excitation field coupling and optical aberrations at the plasmonic tip apex.
main_result: Implementing adaptive wavefront shaping using Zernike modes allows for systematic field optimization, leading to significant TERS signal enhancements of 5 to 15 fold compared to conventional methods.
method: A sequential feedback algorithm is employed, iteratively adjusting the wavefront via a Spatial Light Modulator (SLM) based on either the near-field s-SNOM signal or the intensity of a specific Raman band.
model_or_system: The experiment uses a plasmonic tip (e.g., Ag tip) coupled with a high-NA objective for near-field spectroscopy (s-SNOM/TERS) on samples like Janus MoSSe monolayers.
key_observables: Raman enhancement factor (E_F), s-SNOM signal intensity, and the structured phase/intensity distribution at the back-pupil plane.
important_parameters: Zernike mode coefficients, objective numerical aperture (1.3 N.A.), and the two-step optimization sequence (s-SNOM $ightarrow$ Raman band).
main_assumptions: The Zernike mode basis provides a physically interpretable and comprehensive control over the wavefront, allowing for both aberration correction and active field engineering.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the experimental setup, the simulated evolution of the optical intensity profile along the z-axis, the phase pattern generated by the SLM, and the resulting structured intensity distribution at the back-pupil plane.
paper_structure: The paper details the setup, presents the methodology of adaptive wavefront shaping using Zernike modes, demonstrates the optimization process using s-SNOM signals, and culminates in a two-step optimization strategy using the Raman signal for maximum TERS enhancement.
why_interesting: The work demonstrates advanced control over light fields at the nanoscale using wavefront shaping, which has direct implications for developing next-generation quantum optical sensors or characterization tools.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Fig. 2: Adaptive optimization and stabilization of the longitudinal near field.
